items.title: Designing a Multi-Tenant Hub-and-Spoke Architecture in Azure
items.author: Mahmoud Khatib
items.content:
Centralized VPN Access for Multi-Tenant Azure Environment

This article is aimed at cloud architects and IT professionals designing secure, scalable Azure environments. It focuses on implementing a multi-tenant hub-and-spoke architecture aligned with Microsoft’s Cloud Adoption Framework, offering practical guidance for workload separation and shared service integration.

In modern cloud architectures, particularly within large enterprises or organizations with strict compliance requirements, implementing a multi-tenant Azure environment is becoming increasingly essential. In this context, I was tasked with designing and integrating multiple Azure tenants into an existing hub-and-spoke topology. In this model, the central “hub” acts as the Azure Platform Landing Zone, providing shared services and foundational infrastructure, while each “spoke” represents a separate Azure tenant or Application Landing Zone. These spoke tenants are purpose-built to host specific workloads and applications, with resource configurations tailored to their unique requirements.

This architecture allows for effective workload isolation — commonly used to separate staging and production environments — while still enabling access to centralized shared services. It provides a secure, scalable, and manageable way to support various application lifecycles and business units.

In this article, we will explore the theoretical design of a multi-tenant hub-and-spoke topology in Azure, examine how tenants are connected, and demonstrate how end users can access resources across both staging and production environments. This architectural approach aligns closely with the Microsoft Cloud Adoption Framework (CAF) for Azure, and represents a best practice for organizations aiming to optimize governance, security, and operational efficiency.

2. What is Azure Landing Zone (ALZ):

Let’s first explain what an Azure landing zone [1] is before diving deeper, as Azure’s architecture can be more complex to understand compared to AWS.

An Azure Landing Zone, as defined by the Microsoft Cloud Adoption Framework (CAF) [2], is a foundational cloud environment designed to host your workloads securely, efficiently, and at scale. It follows key design principles across eight critical areas:

  1. Azure Billing and Microsoft Entra ID Tenant: How your Azure subscriptions relate to your Microsoft Entra ID [3] (formerly Azure Active Directory) tenants.
  2. Identity and Access Management: Centralized identity and access control.
  3. Management Group and Subscription Organization: A hierarchical structure for governance.
  4. Network Topology and Connectivity: Hub-and-spoke, Virtual WAN, ExpressRoute, VPN.
  5. Implementing security controls, policies, and monitoring.
  6. Operations, monitoring, patching, and backup.
  7. Policy, compliance, cost management.
  8. Platform Automation and DevOps: Infrastructure as Code (IaC) for consistent deployments.

The below diagram is the high-level design that we will go through during this article, where we have Azure LZ platform act as a hub connected to two Azure tenants acting as a spoke.

Let is go through each component individually:

3.1. The Hub Tenant- Your Azure Landing Zone Platform:

This tenant is the central control plane and shared services provider. It will host the core “platform” subscriptions and resources as defined by the Azure Landing Zone architecture. Below are the ey Services in the Hub Tenant (Platform Landing Zone):

3.1.1. Centralized Networking:

The core of your hub-and-spoke.
3.1.1.2.Azure Firewall [4] : For centralized network security, routing, and traffic inspection between spokes and to/from on-premises/internet.
For hybrid connectivity to your on-premises data centers.
3.1.1.4.Azure Virtual WAN (Optional but Recommended for Scale): If you anticipate a very large number of spoke tenants, or complex global connectivity needs, Azure Virtual WAN can simplify routing, security (with vWAN Hub Firewall), and branch connectivity significantly.
3.1.1.5. Azure Private DNS Zones: Crucial for Private Endpoint resolution across your peered VNets. Link these to your hub VNet and spokes.

3.1.2. Centralized Identity: Your Microsoft Entra ID instance in the hub tenant will be the primary identity provider. If you have on-premises AD, Azure AD Connect will synchronize identities here.

3.1.3. Centralized Management & Monitoring:

3.1.3.1. Azure Log Analytics Workspace: Collects logs from all connected spokes (via Azure Lighthouse integration).
Centralized dashboards, alerts, and metrics.
3.1.3.3. Microsoft Sentinel: SIEM for security analytics across all tenants.
Enforced at the management group level within the hub tenant, it will govern naming conventions, tagging, allowed resource types, and other compliance requirements. Through Azure Lighthouse, some policies can be extended to managed (spoke) tenants.
3.1.3.5. Security Center (Defender for Cloud): Centralized security posture management and threat protection.

3.2. The Spoke Tenants: Staging and Production Application Landing Zones

We will have at least two additional Azure tenants, specifically dedicated to your staging and production workloads. This provides the highest level of isolation for identity and resource management.

3.2.1. Spoke Tenant A: Staging Tenant:

Dedicated to pre-production testing, UAT, performance testing.
3.2.1.2. Microsoft Entra ID: While your main identity is in the hub, this tenant has its own Microsoft Entra ID instance. For management, you’ll primarily use Azure Lighthouse. If specific application identities need to be distinct per tenant, that can be configured here.
3.2.1.3. Subscription(s) within Staging Tenant: One or more subscriptions for your staging workloads.
Staging VNet(s) peered to the Hub VNet in the hub tenant.
Deploy your application’s staging environment (VMs, App Services, AKS, databases, etc.) within this tenant’s VNet.
Apply network security groups (NSGs) for granular control within the staging VNet. Policies pushed from the hub will apply.
Access to resources in the staging tenant is strictly controlled, often with different RBAC assignments than production.

3.2.2. Spoke Tenant B: Production Tenant

Dedicated to live, mission-critical production workloads.
3.2.2.2. Microsoft Entra ID: Similar to staging, this has its own Microsoft Entra ID.
3.2.2.3. Subscription(s) within Production Tenant: One or more subscriptions for your production workloads.
Production VNet(s) peered to the Hub VNet in the hub tenant.
Deploy your live application environment here.
Implement the highest level of security controls (WAFs, stricter NSGs, Private Endpoints, dedicated Key Vault for production secrets, etc.). Policies from the hub will be enforced, potentially with additional production-specific policies.
Access to production resources is highly restricted, adhering to the principle of least privilege, often requiring just-in-time (JIT) access or privileged identity management (PIM) for elevated roles.

3.3. Cross-Tenant Integration (The Glue)

3.3.1. Azure Lighthouse (Crucial for Operations):
Your will be the .
Your and will be .
Onboard subscriptions from the staging and production tenants to Azure Lighthouse. This allows your central operations team (from the hub tenant) to manage, monitor, and enforce policies on resources in these spoke tenants.
Centralized monitoring (Log Analytics, Sentinel), centralized policy enforcement, simplified cross-tenant automation, reduced need for guest accounts or switching contexts.

3.3.2. Virtual Network Peering:

Hub VNet (in Hub Tenant) ↔ Staging VNet (in Staging Tenant)
Hub VNet (in Hub Tenant) ↔ Production VNet (in Production Tenant)
Ensure no overlapping IP address ranges across VNets (Hub, Staging, Production).

3.4. Multi-Tenant approach Vs One-Tenant and multiple Vnets:

  • Provides a high level of isolation for identity and access management. A breach in the staging tenant’s Microsoft Entra ID doesn’t automatically compromise the production tenant’s Microsoft Entra ID.
  • Clear billing separation per environment (tenant).
  • Meets stringent compliance requirements that mandate complete separation of production environments.
  • Limits the impact of misconfigurations or security incidents to a specific environment.
  • Delegated Administration: Empowers different teams (e.g., development teams for staging, operations teams for production) to have more autonomy within their respective tenants, while central IT maintains overall control via Azure Lighthouse.

Note: This is similar to how AWS can be structured — either with multiple accounts for each workload or with a single VPC hosting multiple workloads

ess for Multi-Tenant Azure Environment

Imagine you have an Azure environment where different departments operate within their own spoke virtual networks (VNets). Each spoke VNet contains resources specific to that tenant, such as virtual machines, databases, and applications. To provide secure remote access for end-users to these resources, you can implement a centralized VPN solution in a .

Below diagram shows the architecture:

The diagram consists of the below components:

This VNet acts as the central point for all incoming VPN connections. It hosts an Azure VPN Gateway (Point-to-Site configuration) capable of handling multiple concurrent connections. The hub VNet can also host shared services like Active Directory Domain Services (AD DS) for authentication, or security appliances like Azure Firewall for centralized traffic inspection.
Each department or client has its own dedicated spoke VNet, peered with the hub VNet. This peering allows traffic to flow between the hub and the spokes.
C. This is a specialized network device or service that creates secure, encrypted tunnels over the public internet. It’s crucial for establishing VPN connections. All remote connections will land here first before accessing the private resources.
End-users connect to the Azure VPN Gateway in the hub VNet using VPN client software configured with the appropriate connection profiles.
E. Azure Cross-Tenant Vnets peering: This is theVNet peering is a networking mechanism in Azure that allows you to seamlessly connect two or more Azure VNets. Once peered, the virtual networks appear as one for connectivity purposes.

The diagram below shows the end-to-end traffic flow for the remote user:

1- VPN The remote user will use its VPN client software to connect to the VPN in order to get a private IP within the configured range in order to connect to the private resources that the remote user authorized to connect to.

2- Before Azure accept the P2S connection, the remote user has to be authenticated first. There are two mechanisms that Azure offers to authenticate a connecting user:

2.1. Certificate authentication: using the native Azure certificate authentication.
2.2. Microsoft Entra authentication: Allow users to connect to their Microsoft Entra credentials. The remote user need to use Azure VPN client to connect to Azure resources. With this method, you can user Microsoft Entra conditional access and MFA features for VPN.
2.3. Allows you to leverage an external RADIUS server (on premise or cloud)for user authentication when connecting to your Azure virtual network. This mechanism for organizations that already have an existing RADIUS infrastructure and they want to use it as an authentication point for all VPN connections.

Let is choose Microsoft Entra as the authentication mechanism for this workflow, then the authentication process will be as below steps:

2.2.1. VPN Client Initialization: The user launches the Azure VPN Client application on their device. The client is pre-configured with a profile downloaded from the Azure VPN Gateway, which contains information about the Entra ID tenant, audience, and issuer.
2.2.2. Redirect to Microsoft Entra ID: When the user initiates a connection, the Azure VPN Client redirects the authentication request to Microsoft Entra ID.
Microsoft Entra ID prompts the user to sign in using their organizational credentials (username and password).
2.2.4. Multi-Factor Authentication (if configured).
2.2.5. Conditional Access Evaluation (if configured): Microsoft Entra ID evaluates any applicable Conditional Access policies. If the user’s sign-in attempt doesn’t meet the policy requirements (e.g., they’re signing in from an untrusted location or on a non-compliant device), access is denied.
If authentication and any Conditional Access policies are successful, Microsoft Entra ID issues an OAuth 2.0 access token to the Azure VPN Client. This token contains claims about the authenticated user.
2.2.7. Token Presentation to VPN Gateway: The Azure VPN Client presents this access token to the Azure VPN Gateway.
2.2.8. Token Validation by VPN Gateway: The Azure VPN Gateway validates the access token with Microsoft Entra ID (or by verifying its signature and claims locally, relying on trust established during configuration). It ensures the token is valid, hasn’t expired, and was issued for the correct audience.
2.2.9. Connection Establishment: If the token is valid and the user is authorized (based on any assigned groups or roles), the Azure VPN Gateway establishes the P2S VPN tunnel, and the client is assigned an IP address from the configured client address pool.

3. Authorization and control process: To configure authorization and control which remote users can connect to specific resource (Azure VMs) when using Entra ID for Azure P2S VPN, you have several options:

- User and Group Assignment: You can register a custom application in your Entra ID and assign specific users or groups to this application.
- Network Security Groups (NSGs): After users connect to the VPN, you can use NSGs to control the network traffic to and from specific subnets or VMs. By creating NSG rules, you can allow VPN users to access only the VMs they are authorized to use.
- User-Defined Routes (UDRs): For more granular control, you can use UDRs to route traffic from specific IP ranges (representing the VPN client address pool) only to the authorized subnets.
Entra ID Conditional Access policies let you enforce more stringent access requirements, such as multi-factor authentication (MFA), device compliance, or location-based restrictions, before allowing VPN connections. This adds an extra layer of security.

5. Virtual Network Gateways Routing in Azure

The decision to have connectivity via or VPN Gateway with Azure Firewall depends entirely on your security requirements, compliance needs. There are two options:

5. 1. VPN Gateway Only (No Azure Firewall):

This is a good option for a basic Connectivity, where the VPN Gateway establishes a secure, encrypted tunnel between your Azure VNet(s) and your on-premises network or another Vnets via peering. In this case, you would rely heavily on NSGs applied to subnets and NICs within your Azure VNets for basic traffic filtering (allowing/denying traffic based on IP address, port, and protocol).

5.2. VPN Gateway with Azure Firewall (Recommended for most production environments):

In this option we will have Handles the secure, encrypted tunnel for cross-premises connectivity and an cloud-native network security service that provides, acts as a central point for all inbound and outbound traffic inspection, including traffic between Azure VNets (spoke-to-spoke), traffic to/from on-premises, and traffic to/from the internet, and Advanced Threat Protection (Threat Intelligence, IDS/IPS, URL Filtering), and Application-level Filtering, and Centralized Logging and Monitoring Integrates with Azure Monitor for comprehensive logging, auditing, and alerting.

Note: By default, traffic from Azure Point-to-Site (P2S) VPN clients connected to an Azure VPN Gateway will not automatically flow through a firewall (like Azure Firewall or a Network Virtual Appliance) in a hub VNet, even if that firewall is present in the hub. To force the traffic to go via the firewall, you need to implement User-Defined Routes (UDRs) in specific subnets to redirect traffic to your firewall. This is a common pattern in a hub-and-spoke architecture.

The diagram below showing the use case of combination Azure VPN Gateway and Azure firewall, where we will put the firewall in the packet flow of the remote end user to the destination Azure resource.

6. Using Azure Virtual Hub:

There is another use case where we can use Azure virtual hub instead of Azure Vnet peering to connect the different Vnets in multiple tenants together, see [7] and we can secure the virtual hub with Azure firewall manager

The diagram below shows the use on Azure Virtual Hub as a central point of network connectivity:

Designing a secure and scalable multi-tenant Azure environment using a hub-and-spoke topology is essential for organizations that prioritize workload isolation, centralized governance, and operational efficiency. By leveraging Azure Landing Zones, cross-tenant peering, and services like Azure Lighthouse and VPN Gateway, you can create a robust architecture that supports both production and staging environments (and more teanats) while maintaining strict security and compliance standards. Whether using traditional VNets or advanced options like Azure Virtual WAN/Hub, this approach aligns with Microsoft’s Cloud Adoption Framework (CAF) and sets a solid foundation for future growth, centralized management, and secure remote access across your enterprise cloud environment.

items.title: PostgresML on GKE: Unlocking Deployment for ML Engineers by Fixing the publicly available image’s…
items.author: Mohamed Rasvi
items.content:
PostgresML on GKE: Unlocking Deployment for ML Engineers by Fixing the publicly available image’s Startup Bug

As a Platform Engineer and DevOps practitioner, my core mission is to empower development teams, including our brilliant Machine Learning (ML) engineers, to deploy and operate their applications seamlessly. This often involves wrestling with infrastructure, containerization, and the subtle nuances of cloud-native environments. Recently, we faced a compelling challenge when deploying PostgresML an exciting open-source library that integrates ML directly into PostgreSQL onto our Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) clusters.

This journey became a prime example of why MLOps isn’t just about automation, but also about deep collaboration and methodical troubleshooting.

The Challenge: A Mysterious Dashboard Failure for ML Workflows

Our ML engineers were eager to leverage PostgresML to streamline their model training and inference directly within our data layer. The plan was to deploy PostgresML as a StatefulSet on GKE, integrating it with our existing MLOps stack, which utilizes Airflow for orchestration and Kubernetes PodController jobs for task execution.

They had crafted initial Kubernetes manifests, anticipating a smooth ride with the official ghcr.io/postgresml/postgresml:2.10.0 Docker image. However, upon deployment, the PostgresML dashboard, a critical UI for managing models, remained stubbornly inaccessible. Browser attempts consistently hit an ERR_EMPTY_RESPONSE, leaving them at a crossroads: was it a GKE network misconfiguration, a firewall issue, or something deeper within the application itself?

This is where I, as a Platform Engineer, stepped in. My role shifted from enabling a straightforward deployment to becoming a detective, meticulously peeling back layers to understand the core problem.

The DevOps Debugging Playbook: From Cloud to Local

To isolate the issue, my first instinct was to replicate the problem locally. I spun up the PostgresML container on a developer’s machine using docker run, mirroring the essential port mappings and persistent volume:

docker run \  -v postgresml_data:/var/lib/postgresql \  -p 8000:8000 \
  ghcr.io/postgresml/postgresml:2.10.0

Initial docker logs showed promising messages: "Starting PostgreSQL 15 database server...done." and "Starting dashboard." Yet, the container would immediately exit. A quick docker ps -a confirmed Exited (0). This suggested the primary process wasn't staying alive. To keep the container running for deeper inspection, I resorted to adding tail -f /dev/null to the command, forcing the container to stay up:

docker run -d \  -v postgresml_data:/var/lib/postgresql \  -p 8000:8000 \
  ghcr.io/postgresml/postgresml:2.10.0 \
  tail -f /dev/null

With the container now Up and running in the background, I attempted to curl the dashboard from my host machine:

curl -v http://localhost:8000

* Connected to localhost (127.0.0.1) port 8000...
* Empty reply from server
curl: (52) Empty reply from server

The Empty reply from server was a critical clue. It indicated that a connection , but the server (the dashboard application) sent no data back. This strongly suggested an application-level issue rather than a networking or firewall block.

To drill down further, I used docker exec to get a shell inside the running container and performed internal checks:

docker exec -it <CONTAINER_ID> bash

My first attempt to check listening ports with netstat -tulnp | grep 8000 failed due to netstat: command not found. After a quick apt-get update and a (failing) apt-get install attempt for net-tools due to an unrelated repository issue, I immediately tried to curl the dashboard from within the container itself:

root@<container_id>:/# curl -v http://localhost:8000* Trying 127.0.0.1:8000...
* connect to 127.0.0.1 port 8000 failed: Connection refused
...
curl: (7) Failed to connect to localhost port 8000 after 9 ms: Connection refused

This was the decisive moment. A Connection refused from inside the container to its own localhost confirmed unequivocally that the pgml-dashboard process was on port 8000. The docker logs message "Starting dashboard" was misleading; it only indicated that the to start the dashboard was executed, not that the dashboard successfully initialized and began serving traffic.

The Smoking Gun: A Zombie Process (ps aux)

With netstat unavailable, my ultimate diagnostic tool was ps aux from inside the container, to see what processes were actually running:

root@<container_id>:/# ps aux

And there it was, the definitive proof:

root           70  0.0  0.0      0     0 ?        Z    <timestamp>   0:00 [pgml-dashboard] <defunct>

This [pgml-dashboard] <defunct> entry, coupled with the Z status, revealed a . This means:

  • The pgml-dashboard executable was indeed launched.
  • It crashed or exited almost immediately after being launched.
  • The parent process (the entrypoint.sh script) failed to properly reap its exit status, leaving a "ghost" process in the system's table.

This explained everything. The dashboard wasn’t running because it was crashing immediately upon startup, leaving no active service to respond to any incoming connections.

The Platform Engineering Solution: A Robust GKE Manifest

With the root cause identified as an internal application startup issue within the official PostgresML Docker image, we could confidently inform the ML team that GKE itself was not the problem. The solution lay in creating a Kubernetes manifest that takes explicit control over the container’s lifecycle and processes, moving beyond the assumptions of the default image entrypoint.

For a stateful application like PostgresML, a StatefulSet with an associated Service was crucial. Here's a breakdown of the key elements we implemented in our manifest (using 2.9.3 which we found to be more stable for this custom setup):

# Kubernetes Service to expose PostgreSQL and Dashboard ports# ClusterIP: None is essential for StatefulSets' stable network identitieskind: Service  name: postgresml-service    app: postgresml  clusterIP: None    app: postgresml    - name: postgres      targetPort: 5432      port: 8000---
# StatefulSet for persistent PostgresML deployment
apiVersion: apps/v1metadata:
  name: postgresml-statefulset
  labels:spec:
  serviceName: "postgresml-service"
  replicas: 1    matchLabels:  template:      labels:    spec:
      # Set fsGroup for correct volume ownership (common for postgres user)
      securityContext:      initContainers:
        # This initContainer handles PostgreSQL data directory initialization and permissions.
        # It's crucial for ensuring data persistence and correct ownership on the volume.
        - name: initialize-postgres-data
          image: ghcr.io/postgresml/postgresml:2.9.3            - sh            - |
              # Ensure data directory and permissions are correct
              mkdir -p /var/lib/postgresql
              chown -R postgres:postgres /var/lib/postgresql
              mkdir -p /var/lib/postgresql/15/main
              # Initialize PostgreSQL cluster if not already done
              if [ ! -s /var/lib/postgresql/15/main/PG_VERSION ]; then
                echo "PostgreSQL data directory is not initialized or invalid. Attempting to create/recreate cluster..."
                if pg_lsclusters | grep -q "15 main"; then
                  echo "Stale cluster configuration '15/main' found. Dropping it..."
                  pg_dropcluster 15 main --stop || true                fi
                find /var/lib/postgresql/15/main -mindepth 1 -delete || true
                echo "Cleaned up /var/lib/postgresql/15/main directory contents."
                echo "Creating PostgreSQL cluster 'main' for version 15..."
                pg_createcluster 15 main --datadir=/var/lib/postgresql/15/main
                echo "PostgreSQL cluster '15/main' created and initialized."
              else
                echo "PostgreSQL data directory already exists and is initialized. Skipping creation."
              fi
              chown -R postgres:postgres /var/lib/postgresql/15/main
              chmod 0700 /var/lib/postgresql/15/main            - name: postgresml-data-db
              mountPath: /var/lib/postgresql
      containers:          image: ghcr.io/postgresml/postgresml:2.9.3 # Consistent image version
          # IMPORTANT: Override default ENTRYPOINT/CMD to directly control startup.
          # This ensures that `pgml-dashboard` is the main, foreground process.          args:              set -e
              echo "Starting PostgresML (direct pg_ctl method)"
              mkdir -p /var/log/postgresql
              chown postgres:postgres /var/log/postgresql
              chmod 0700 /var/lib/postgresql/15/main
              sudo -u postgres /usr/lib/postgresql/15/bin/pg_ctl start \
                -D /var/lib/postgresql/15/main \
                -l /var/log/postgresql/postgresql-15-main.log \
                -s \
                -o "-c config_file=/etc/postgresql/15/main/postgresql.conf"
              echo "Waiting for PostgreSQL to be ready..."
              until sudo -u postgres pg_isready -h localhost -p 5432; do
                echo "PostgreSQL is unavailable - sleeping"
                sleep 1              echo "PostgreSQL is up and running!"
              echo "Setting up PostgresML users and database..."
              useradd postgresml -m 2> /dev/null 1>&2 || true
              sudo -u postgresml touch /home/postgresml/.psql_history || true
              sudo -u postgres createuser root --superuser --login 2> /dev/null 1>&2 || true
              sudo -u postgres psql -c "ALTER ROLE postgresml WITH PASSWORD 'yourpassword'" 2>/dev/null || \
              sudo -u postgres psql -c "CREATE ROLE postgresml PASSWORD 'yourpassword' SUPERUSER LOGIN"
              sudo -u postgres createdb postgresml --owner postgresml 2> /dev/null 1>&2 || true
              sudo -u postgres psql -c 'ALTER ROLE postgresml SET search_path TO public,pgml' 2> /dev/null 1>&2 || true
              echo "Starting dashboard directly"
              export DATABASE_URL=postgres://postgresml:yourpassword@127.0.0.1:5432/postgresml
              export DASHBOARD_STATIC_DIRECTORY=/usr/share/pgml-dashboard/dashboard-static
              export DASHBOARD_CMS_DIRECTORY=/usr/share/pgml-cms
              export SEARCH_INDEX_DIRECTORY=/var/lib/pgml-dashboard/search-index
              export ROCKET_SECRET_KEY=$(openssl rand -hex 32)
              export ROCKET_ADDRESS=0.0.0.0 # Bind to all interfaces for external access
              export RUST_LOG=info
              export SITE_SEARCH_DATABASE_URL=postgres://postgresml:yourpassword@127.0.0.1:5432/postgresml
              # CRUCIAL: 'exec' replaces the current shell process with pgml-dashboard,
              # making it the main process of the container. Errors will be visible.
              exec /usr/bin/pgml-dashboard 
          ports:            - containerPort: 8000            - name: POSTGRES_PASSWORD          volumeMounts:
            - name: postgresml-data-db
              mountPath: /var/lib/postgresql            requests:              memory: 4Gi              cpu: "4"  volumeClaimTemplates:        name: postgresml-data-db        accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]          requests:        storageClassName: "standard"

Lessons for DevOps and ML Collaboration

This experience profoundly illustrated key aspects of modern MLOps and platform engineering:

  • Shared Responsibility & Expertise: The initial problem for the ML team was a deployment issue, but the solution required deep container runtime knowledge, a domain of DevOps/Platform Engineers. Collaboration was paramount.
  • The Nuance of “Dockerized”: Just because an application has a Docker image doesn’t mean it’s immediately “Kubernetes-ready” for robust, production-grade deployments. Understanding the image’s ENTRYPOINT/CMD behavior is critical.
  • Sometimes, docker logs or kubectl logs don't tell the full story. Debugging inside the container with tools like ps aux, curl localhost, and understanding process states (like zombies) is indispensable.
  • Platform Patterns for Stateful Apps: Stateful applications, especially databases, demand careful management of persistent storage, initialization, and process lifecycles within Kubernetes. StatefulSets, initContainers, and explicit command/args are standard patterns for this.
  • Enabling, Not Just Deploying: Our role as Platform Engineers is to remove obstacles and provide robust, self-healing platforms. By solving this underlying image issue, we empowered our ML engineers to focus on their core task: building and deploying innovative ML models with PostgresML.

items.title: A Scalable Approach to FastAPI Projects with PostgreSQL, Alembic, Pytest, and Docker Using uv
items.author: Arafat Hussain
items.content:
FastAPI is a modern, high-performance web framework that makes it easy to build APIs. However, as projects grow, : a clear project layout (with separate modules for configuration, models, routes, etc.) makes your code more maintainable and scalable. In this tutorial, we’ll use  — a new fast Python package manager — to scaffold and manage our project. UV is designed for speed and reproducibility: it uses a pyproject.toml and a uv.lock file to lock dependencies, automatically handles virtual environments, and caches aggressively for fast installs. In fact, the FastAPI team now recommends uv for managing FastAPI projects.

We’ll build a simple User Management CRUD API step-by-step. The API will let us create, retrieve, update, and delete users, with data stored in PostgreSQL. We’ll use SQLAlchemy for database access, Alembic for migrations, Pytest + HTTPX for testing, and Docker (with Docker Compose) for containerization. Each step below covers code examples and explanations to help beginners follow along.

Step 1: Project Initialization with uv

First, install uv (see uv docs for your platform). Then open a terminal and initialize a new project:

uv init --app my_projectcd my_project

The --app flag tells uv to create an (including an app/ directory) and a pyproject.toml file. Next, add FastAPI (including all recommended extras) as a dependency:

uv add fastapi --extra standard

We’ll organize our project using the following structure:

my_project/├── app/│   │   ├── routes/
│   │   │   └── __init__.py
│   │   └── __init__.py│   │   ├── __init__.py│   ├── database/│   │   └── init_db.py│   │   ├── __init__.py
│   │   └── http_exception.py
│   ├── models/│   │   └── user.py│   │   ├── __init__.py│   ├── services/│   │   └── user_service.py├── migrations/│   ├── env.py├── tests/│   └── test_user.py├── Dockerfile├── pyproject.toml├── alembic.ini

Next, edit app/main.py to add a simple health check endpoint. This verifies that our server is running:

# app/main.pyfrom fastapi import FastAPIdef health_check():
    return {"status": "healthy"}
if __name__ == "__main__":
    import uvicorn
    uvicorn.run(app, host="0.0.0.0", port=8000)

You can run the server immediately with uv. For example, the Astral docs show using uv run fastapi dev, which starts a development server. Alternatively, you can invoke uvicorn directly:

uv run uvicorn app.main:app --reload --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000

The uv run command will install (or sync) dependencies, create a virtual environment, lock packages, and run the given command inside that environment. Once the server is running, you can visit http://127.0.0.1:8000/health in your browser or use curl to see the JSON {"status": "healthy"} response.

Step 2: Configuration and Environment

Next, we set up configuration. We’ll use Pydantic’s settings management to load environment variables from a .env file. First, create a folder and file for settings:

Create app/core/config.py with a Settings class:

# app/core/config.pyfrom pydantic_settings import BaseSettings, SettingsConfigDict
class Settings(BaseSettings):
    database_url: str
    # Tell Pydantic to load environment variables from .env
    model_config = SettingsConfigDict(env_file=".env")

Here, database_url will come from an environment variable. We also add model_config = SettingsConfigDict(env_file=".env") so that Pydantic reads a .env file (this requires python-dotenv to be installed). Install the needed packages:

uv add pydantic-settings python-dotenv

Now create a .env file in the project root to hold sensitive or variable settings. For local development with PostgreSQL, you might write:

# .envDATABASE_URL=postgresql://postgres:postgres@user-db:5432/user_db

If you prefer not to set up a local PostgreSQL instance, you can use a cloud-based provider like to manage your database. Here’s a concise guide to obtain a connection URL from Neon and configure it in your .env file:

Using Neon as Your PostgreSQL Provider

First, visit Neon and sign up for an account. After logging in, click on , provide a project name, select a region, and create the project.

Once your project is set up, navigate to the . Click the button to open the “Connect to your database” modal. In this modal, select the desired branch, compute, database, and role. A connection string will be generated for you. Keep this string in .env file.

This defines DATABASE_URL, which our Settings class will load. FastAPI’s documentation recommends using python-dotenv so that Pydantic’s BaseSettings can read .env files. By installing python-dotenv and adding the above model_config line, running Settings() will load DATABASE_URL from .env.

Step 3: Database Integration with SQLAlchemy

We’ll use SQLAlchemy for ORM. Create a new file for database setup:

In app/database/init_db.py, initialize the engine, session maker, and base class:

# app/database/init_db.pyfrom sqlalchemy import create_engine
from sqlalchemy.orm import sessionmaker, declarative_base
from app.core.config import settings
# Create the SQLAlchemy engine using the database URL from config
engine = create_engine(settings.database_url)
# Create a configured "SessionLocal" class
SessionLocal = sessionmaker(autocommit=False, autoflush=False, bind=engine)
# Base class for our models
Base = declarative_base()    """Create all tables in the database."""
    Base.metadata.create_all(bind=engine)
    db = SessionLocal()        yield db        db.close()

This follows the common pattern: we call create_engine() with our DATABASE_URL, then define SessionLocal = sessionmaker(...) for session instances. We also define a helper create_tables() that will generate the tables from our models.

This get_db() generator yields a database session for use in FastAPI routes. It ensures the session is closed after the request. This pattern (create a session, yield it, then close it) is standard for FastAPI + SQLAlchemy. We’ll use Depends(get_db) in our route functions.

Step 4: Models and Schemas

Now let’s define the User model and corresponding Pydantic schemas.

Create the SQLAlchemy model User in app/models/user.py:

# app/models/user.pyfrom sqlalchemy import Column, Integer, String
from app.database.init_db import Base
    __tablename__ = "users"
    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True, index=True)
    name = Column(String, index=True)
    email = Column(String, unique=True, index=True)

This User model maps to a users table with an integer id primary key, a name, and a unique email.

For request/response validation, create Pydantic schemas in app/schemas/user.py:

# app/schemas/user.pyfrom pydantic import BaseModel
class UserBase(BaseModel):
    name: str
class UserCreate(UserBase):
    pass  # In a real app you might add password or other fields here
class UserRead(UserBase):
    id: int        orm_mode = True

Here, UserBase has the shared fields. UserCreate inherits it (no extra fields for now), and UserRead adds id. Setting orm_mode = True in Config lets Pydantic read data from SQLAlchemy objects. In FastAPI, we often use one set of Pydantic classes (schemas) for request validation and response models, separate from the SQLAlchemy models. As one answer notes: “The models data classes define the SQL tables, and the schemas define the API that FastAPI uses to interact with the database”. This clear separation keeps our code organized.

Step 5: User Service Layer

To keep our code organized, implement the user-related database logic in a service module. Create app/services/user.py with functions for each CRUD operation:

# app/services/user.pyfrom sqlalchemy.orm import Session
from app.models.user import User
from app.schemas.user import UserCreate
def create_user(db: Session, user: UserCreate) -> User:
    new_user = User(name=user.name, email=user.email)    db.commit()    return new_user
def get_user(db: Session, user_id: int) -> User | None:
    return db.query(User).filter(User.id == user_id).first()
def get_user_by_email(db: Session, email: str) -> User | None:
    return db.query(User).filter(User.email == email).first()
def get_users(db: Session, skip: int = 0, limit: int = 100) -> list[User]:
    return db.query(User).offset(skip).limit(limit).all()
def update_user(db: Session, user_id: int, user: UserCreate) -> User | None:
    db_user = get_user(db, user_id)        return None    db_user.email = user.email    db.refresh(db_user)
def delete_user(db: Session, user_id: int) -> bool:
    db_user = get_user(db, user_id)        return False    db.commit()

Each function takes a database session and operates on the User model. For example, create_user adds and commits a new user; get_user fetches by ID; update_user changes fields; and delete_user removes a user. By putting this logic in a , our route code (Step 6) can remain clean and focused on HTTP stuff. This also makes unit testing easier, as you can test service functions separately.

Step 6: API Routes and Custom Exceptions

Now we create the FastAPI routes that use our service layer. First, define custom HTTP exceptions in app/exceptions/http_exceptions.py:

# app/exceptions/http_exceptions.pyfrom fastapi import HTTPException, status
class UserNotFoundException(HTTPException):
    def __init__(self, detail: str = "User not found"):
        super().__init__(status_code=status.HTTP_404_NOT_FOUND, detail=detail)
class EmailAlreadyExistsException(HTTPException):
    def __init__(self, detail: str = "Email already exists"):
        super().__init__(status_code=status.HTTP_400_BAD_REQUEST, detail=detail)

These subclasses of HTTPException let us raise clear error conditions in one place.

Next, add route definitions in app/api/routes/user.py. We’ll create a router for /users endpoints:

# app/api/routes/user.pyfrom typing import List
from fastapi import APIRouter, Depends
from sqlalchemy.orm import Session
from app.schemas.user import UserCreate, UserRead
from app.services.user import create_user, get_user, get_users, update_user, delete_user, get_user_by_email
from app.exceptions.http_exceptions import UserNotFoundException, EmailAlreadyExistsException
from app.database.init_db import get_db
router = APIRouter(prefix="/users", tags=["users"])
@router.get("/", response_model=List[UserRead])
def list_users(skip: int = 0, limit: int = 100, db: Session = Depends(get_db)):
    return get_users(db, skip=skip, limit=limit)
@router.post("/", response_model=UserRead)
def create_new_user(user: UserCreate, db: Session = Depends(get_db)):
    if get_user_by_email(db, user.email):
        raise EmailAlreadyExistsException()
    return create_user(db, user)
@router.get("/{user_id}", response_model=UserRead)
def read_user(user_id: int, db: Session = Depends(get_db)):
    db_user = get_user(db, user_id)
    if db_user is None:
        raise UserNotFoundException()
    return db_user
@router.put("/{user_id}", response_model=UserRead)
def update_existing_user(user_id: int, user: UserCreate, db: Session = Depends(get_db)):
    updated = update_user(db, user_id, user)
    if updated is None:
        raise UserNotFoundException()
    return updated
@router.delete("/{user_id}")
def delete_existing_user(user_id: int, db: Session = Depends(get_db)):
    success = delete_user(db, user_id)
    if not success:
        raise UserNotFoundException()
    return {"ok": True}
  • We use Depends(get_db) to inject the database session into each route.
  • For creation, we check if the email already exists and raise EmailAlreadyExistsException if so.
  • For fetching/updating/deleting by ID, we raise UserNotFoundException if no user is found.
  • Each route specifies a response_model (like UserRead) so FastAPI will serialize SQLAlchemy models to JSON automatically.

In app/main.py, include this router so the routes become active:

# app/main.py (add after creating FastAPI app)from contextlib import asynccontextmanager
from app.database.init_db import create_tables
from app.api.routes import user as user_routerasync def lifespan(app: FastAPI):
    create_tables()  # Initializes tables (SQLAlchemy)
    print("✅ Application started and database tables created!")    print("🛑 Application shutting down!")
app = FastAPI(lifespan=lifespan)
app.include_router(user_router.router)

This registers all /users routes on our FastAPI app.

We’ll define a lifespan function that creates the database tables when the app starts and logs messages on both startup and shutdown. create_tables() runs when the app starts, ensuring your database schema is ready. The lifespan function uses @asynccontextmanager to manage the app's lifecycle events cleanly.

At this point, you have a fully functional User CRUD API (excluding migrations). You can run the server (uv run uvicorn app.main:app --reload) and use /docs to interact with the endpoints.

Step 7: Database Migrations with Alembic

Raw SQLAlchemy is great, but for a production project we should use migrations to manage schema changes. We’ll set up Alembic to create our users table.

Then initialize migrations:

uv run alembic init migrations

This creates an alembic.ini file and a migrations/ folder. Edit migrations/env.py to configure it:

# migrations/env.py (relevant parts)from alembic import context
from sqlalchemy import engine_from_config
from logging.config import fileConfig
from app.core.config import settings
from app.database.init_db import Base
# Override the database URL in alembic.ini with our settings
config.set_main_option("sqlalchemy.url", settings.database_url)
# Interpret the config file for Python logging.
fileConfig(config.config_file_name)
# Set target_metadata for 'autogenerate' support
target_metadata = Base.metadata... # Keep rest of the code same

Here, we import our settings and override Alembic’s sqlalchemy.url with settings.database_url (read from .env). We also set target_metadata = Base.metadata so Alembic knows about our models. (In a single-module app like this, Base includes all models that inherit from it.)

Now generate the first migration:

uv run alembic revision --autogenerate -m "create users table"

Alembic will detect the User model and generate SQL to create the users table. Finally, apply the migration:

uv run alembic upgrade head

This creates the table in your PostgreSQL database. (You should see the users table in your DB now.) The Alembic docs confirm this workflow: DATABASE_URL variable and setting the , we are configuring Alembic to connect to our database... Create the database migration script and run .

Step 8: API Testing with Pytest

Testing is crucial. We’ll use Pytest and HTTPX’s AsyncClient to test our FastAPI endpoints. First, add test dependencies:

Create a pytest.ini at the project root to tell pytest where to find tests:

# pytest.ini[pytest]

Now create a test file my_project/tests/test_user.py:

# tests/test_user.pyimport pytest
from httpx import AsyncClient, ASGITransport
async def test_health():
    async with AsyncClient(app=app, transport=ASGITransport(app=app), base_url="http://test") as ac:
        res = await ac.get("/health")
    assert res.status_code == 200
    assert res.json() == {"status": "healthy"}async def test_user_crud():
    async with AsyncClient(app=app, transport=ASGITransport(app=app), base_url="http://test") as ac:
        # Create a new user
        create_res = await ac.post("/users/", json={"name": "Alice", "email": "alice@example.com"})
        assert create_res.status_code == 200
        user_data = create_res.json()
        assert user_data["name"] == "Alice"        user_id = user_data["id"]
        # Retrieve the created user
        get_res = await ac.get(f"/users/{user_id}")
        assert get_res.status_code == 200
        fetched = get_res.json()
        assert fetched["email"] == "alice@example.com"
        delete_res = await ac.delete(f"/users/{user_id}")
        assert delete_res.status_code == 200
        assert delete_res.json() == {"ok": True}        get_res2 = await ac.get(f"/users/{user_id}")
        assert get_res2.status_code == 404

Notice we use pytest.mark.anyio and HTTPX’s AsyncClient with ASGITransport to make asynchronous test requests against our FastAPI app. This pattern is recommended in FastAPI’s testing guide for async tests.

Finally, run the tests with:

All tests should pass if your API is set up correctly. Pytest will automatically pick up our tests because of pytest.ini.

Step 9: Dockerizing the Application

To deploy this application, we’ll create a Docker environment. First, a for the FastAPI app. One example (from uv’s docs) is:

# DockerfileFROM python:3.12-slim
# Install uv (Astral Python package manager)
COPY --from=ghcr.io/astral-sh/uv:latest /uv /uvx /bin/COPY . /app
# Install dependencies using uv
RUN uv sync --frozen --no-cacheEXPOSE 8081CMD ["uv", "run", "uvicorn", "app.main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8081"]

This uses uv’s official container image to copy the uv binary into our image. We copy our code, run uv sync to install all dependencies from pyproject.toml and uv.lock, and then set the default command to run uvicorn via uv run.

Next, set up docker-compose.yml to run both the FastAPI app and a PostgreSQL database:

# docker-compose.ymlversion: "3.8"  user-db:    restart: always      POSTGRES_USER: postgres
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: postgres
      POSTGRES_DB: user_db      - user-db-data:/var/lib/postgresql/data      - .env    build: .      - user-db      - "8081:8081"      - .env  user-db-data:
  • The user-db service uses the official Postgres image. Its environment variables match our .env settings (POSTGRES_DB=user_db, etc.).
  • The user-api service builds from our Dockerfile, waits for the database, and maps port 8081. It also reads the same .env so our DATABASE_URL is correct inside the container.
  • We define a named volume user-db-data to persist Postgres data.
# To build and run with Docker:docker-compose builddocker-compose down

This command builds the Docker image for user-api, starts the Postgres container, runs migrations (you might hook that into an entrypoint or run it manually), and launches the FastAPI server inside Docker. You should see logs indicating the FastAPI app is running. Visit http://localhost:8081/health to confirm it works. Thanks to uv, the Docker build is efficient: it locks dependencies and creates the venv inside the image for a repeatable build.

In this tutorial we built a well-structured FastAPI project from scratch, covering:

  • We used a clear layout with app/ for code and separate modules for config, database, models, schemas, services, routes, and exceptions.
  • We used for fast dependency and virtualenv management, and pydantic-settings with python-dotenv to load configuration from .env.
  • We integrated SQLAlchemy with PostgreSQL, setting up an engine, session, and Base, and defined a get_db dependency.
  • We defined a SQLAlchemy User model and Pydantic schemas for API I/O, following best practices (models = DB schema, schemas = request/response).
  • We separated business logic into a user service (app/services/user.py), keeping our routes thin.
  • We created REST endpoints with FastAPI, using dependency injection and custom exceptions (UserNotFoundException, etc.) for error handling.
  • We configured Alembic to manage database migrations, loading the DATABASE_URL from our config and setting target_metadata to our Base metadata.
  • We wrote async tests with Pytest and HTTPX’s AsyncClient to verify our API endpoints.
  • We containerized the app with a Dockerfile using uv and PostgreSQL, and set up Docker Compose to run the API and database together.

This end-to-end setup gives a solid foundation for a real FastAPI project. From here, you can extend the project by adding features like authentication, using SQLAlchemy in async mode, or deploying to a cloud service. But even now, our project follows best practices for structure, modularization, and reproducibility — thanks in part to tools like uv that streamline Python development.

might include adding user authentication (e.g. OAuth2 with JWT), writing more tests (for error cases and edge conditions), switching to async SQLAlchemy/Databases for non-blocking DB calls, or deploying the app to a cloud provider. But for now, you have a complete, tested, containerized FastAPI application ready to go. Happy coding!

items.author: Ahson Shaikh
items.content:
SigNoz is an open-source observability platform built on OpenTelemetry that unifies application performance monitoring (APM), metrics, logs and distributed tracing into a single self-hosted solution GitHub.

  • : Collect and correlate traces, metrics and logs in one pane for end-to-end visibility.
  • : Pinpoint performance bottlenecks across microservices with built-in trace explorer and flamegraphs.
  • Real-Time Metrics & Dashboards: Create custom dashboards and alerts to monitor infrastructure, services and cloud resources.
  • : Query, filter and live-tail logs using an intuitive query builder.
  • : Out-of-the-box support for popular frameworks (Node.js, Java, Python, Go) and back-end stores like ClickHouse SigNoz.

Easypanel is a modern, Docker-powered server control panel that lets you manage and deploy web applications with minimal effort. It functions much like “self-hosted Heroku,” creating runnable Docker images for Node.js, Ruby, Python, PHP, Go and Java apps via Cloud Native Buildpacks — or you can bring your own Dockerfile if you need finer control easypanel.io

  • : Simply push your code to GitHub and Easypanel will automatically build and deploy it.
  • : Provision free, auto-renewing Let’s Encrypt certificates in a single checkbox.
  • Zero-downtime deployments: Updates roll out seamlessly so your users never experience service interruptions.
  • In-browser terminal & logs: Inspect logs or run shell commands without leaving the UI.
  • : Installable with one-click on major providers (DigitalOcean, AWS, Linode, Vultr, Hostinger) or via a simple Docker command on any fresh Linux server
  • Tons of Open-source Softwares: It has more than 400+ templates available of popular applications that can be deployed with just one click.

Post-Installation of Easypanel

After the installation you would have dashboard like this:

Now follow the below given steps to install Signoz on Easypanel.

  1. Create a project and a compose service:

2. Set the GitHub Source with the following details.

3. Click on the Environment section on left side panel:

Enter the value inside the Env, switch toggle to on for “Create .env file” and save and click the green button for deploy.

Select the Signoz Service and Enter the port 8080 and save.

Once it is deployed, you can open the URL in the browser.

items.title: Quality Assurance to Quality Engineering: How to Navigate
items.author: Sourojit Das
items.content:
As software development evolves, the approach to ensuring quality has also transformed. The shift in Quality Engineering vs. Quality Assurance highlights the industry’s move from defect detection to defect prevention, providing faster and more reliable software releases.

This article explores Quality Engineering vs. Quality Assurance, key differences, the shift from QA to QE, industry trends, and how to align your career for future success.

Understanding Quality Assurance

Quality Assurance (QA) is a process-focused approach that ensures software meets predefined quality standards through systematic testing and defect detection. It emphasizes validation, verification, and adherence to best practices to deliver a reliable product.

A tester assuming the persona of a prospective user, using the application, and providing documented feedback is the most commonly accepted role for a QA beginner. This perspective is mainly based on the personal manual part running on documentation and communication, devoid of any significant technical leverage beyond the knowledge of a few popular tools.

Quality assurance was part of industries before the advent of computers. In a traditional waterfall product development workflow, QA is used to manually evaluate products at the end of the production line and certify them for customer use. This perspective came over to the software industry, and initially, the software industry also used to work on waterfall-style development, so this fitted the times appropriately.

But times in the age of computers move much faster. As a result, this model quickly became obsolete with the arrival of things like

  • Object-oriented programming: A new way of encapsulating abstractions to increase modularity and functional separation at the cost of an inflated codebase.
  • Services-based architectures: A way to architect internet resources into packets of business/technical functionality, separated over a net of physical and cloud resources.
  • : Splitting app development into user and server-side parts and developing each using a specially designed tool stack.
  • Cloud technologies and CI/CD: Software as a service, Computation as a service, API as a service, and much more powered by an ever-expanding intertwined network of corporations, open source projects, and institutions.

All these technologies have led to a new emphasis on engineering, especially for software quality.

Understanding Quality Engineering

Quality Engineering (QE) is a proactive approach to software quality. It integrates testing, automation, and DevOps throughout the development lifecycle. Unlike traditional Quality Assurance (QA), which focuses on defect detection, QE prioritizes defect prevention. It ensures quality is embedded at every stage of development.

In the QA vs QE thought process, where anyone with access to the internet can build applications, the factor which often differentiates winners from losers is quality. And quality in the times of iterative product release cycles is not a step before release but a deeply integrated part of the end-to-end development process. Industry in recent years has changed significantly to accommodate fast technological development.

So what this means in practice is that developers have to test and testers have to write code(or at least scripts). A quality engineer is expected to look at the user interaction and the testing operation as a whole and optimize both simultaneously.

It is empowering to look at the current market for CI/CD tools in automated testing and use them to enhance personal and organizational capabilities to achieve new possibilities in comprehension and output.

A QE in practice performs the following tasks:

  • Understand the overall development process in summary and the specifics of the testing part in detail. Equipping yourselves with modern and process-based thinking about testing allows for more measurable value in test design. Design thinking for one is something that is catching up with quality engineering.
  • Capture reports and analytics across all steps and compose a meaningful analysis that will further your data visualization in test reporting.

These are not exclusive or separate things in practice, but in team environments, resources focus on one or the other depending on their technical prowess. Knowing your trajectory in a rapidly growing company is the key to a fulfilling career.

Understanding the concepts outlined in this guide will help you develop a realistic pathway to shift from quality assurance to quality engineering.

Quality Engineering vs Quality Assurance: Key Differences

Understanding the differences between Quality Assurance (QA) and Quality Engineering (QE) is crucial for adapting to modern software development practices. The table below highlights the key differences between the two approaches.

Quality Assurance to Quality Engineering: How to Navigate the Shift

The transition from Quality Assurance (QA) to Quality Engineering (QE) requires a shift in mindset, skills, and approach to software quality. Instead of focusing solely on detecting defects, QE emphasizes preventing defects, automating processes, and integrating testing into the development lifecycle. Here’s how QA professionals can navigate this transition effectively:

  1. Shift from Manual Testing to Automation

2. Embrace CI/CD and DevOps Practices

3. Adopt a Shift-Left Testing Mindset

  • Start testing early in the development cycle to detect issues sooner.
  • Implement unit testing, integration testing, and static code analysis from the beginning.
  • Collaborate closely with developers to enhance code quality.

4. Develop Performance, Security, and Reliability Testing Skills

  • Learn performance testing tools like JMeter and Gatling to ensure scalability.
  • Gain knowledge of security testing principles to prevent vulnerabilities.
  • Focus on reliability testing to improve system stability under different conditions.

5. Stay Updated with Emerging Trends

  • Keep up with industry advancements by following blogs, attending webinars, and obtaining certifications.
  • Engage in hands-on projects to apply QE concepts effectively.

Stay in Sync with Current Industry Standards

Tech industry being well-established worldwide, has its way of testing and evaluating prospective career aspirants. Technology companies within the context of quality assurance vs quality engineering have developed their own relevant evaluation criteria. This is the first thing that an applicant needs to study.

You will find that different companies have different organizational structures and hiring criteria. This is especially clear when you look at the top five tech companies i.e. Apple, Microsoft, Google, Meta and Amazon. They all have different designations and hierarchies, but in principle, they are all tech companies.

A well-studied person understands that all tech team arrangements are subject to updates upon the adoption of newer technologies by the market. So while working within a narrow stream, staying agnostic of titles and tools is an essential skill for someone planning to grow over a long time frame.

Streamline your Aspirations and Goals

What is the ideal work environment you are trying to get into? What kind of trajectory and future growth prospects are you trying to aim towards? A solid vision of these questions will help you gain the momentum you need to work and excel in a competitive environment.

Suppose you do not have confident answers to these questions. In that case, you can level up your overall understanding by:

  • Reading on platforms like this which provide the latest news and solutions to challenges that actual testing teams face.
  • Start implementing projects and practice documentation and scripting.
  • Find out the most relevant technologies for the platform you are interested in and dedicate time to learning + implementation.
  • If you are a student, you need not worry about buying licenses as many popular open-source tools are free for all and are also deployed by actual companies for testing products in production e.g. Selenium, Jmeter
  • Be ready to learn new things. If this seems like a daunting task, then there is some issue with how you are referencing and learning to use new products.
  • With an elevated understanding of modern technologies and products, it will be easier for you to build a plug-in-play mentality with a broad set of tools instead of getting fixated on one tool only.

The shift from Quality Assurance (QA) to Quality Engineering (QE) is essential for modern software development, ensuring quality is built in rather than tested later. QE emphasizes automation, continuous testing, and DevOps integration, leading to faster and more reliable releases.

items.title: When AWS Bedrock Returns “REDACTED”: A Hidden Action Group Pitfall
items.author: Carlos Biagolini-Jr.
items.content:
In December 2024, I encountered a subtle yet frustrating issue while configuring an Amazon Bedrock agent. The problem caused intermittent redaction (<REDACTED>) in model responses, even when the returned content was safe and expected. At the time, I thoroughly searched the AWS documentation, forums, and blogs, but found no reference to this behavior.

I had a breakthrough while explaining Action Groups to a colleague: it dawned on me that the issue might be related to within the Action Groups and their functions. After identifying and resolving the problem, I came across this Reddit thread, where another user described a nearly identical experience. That confirmed the root cause and reinforced the need to document it more visibly for the community.

I built a Bedrock agent that used an Action Group to return Wikipedia URLs based on user queries. The Action Group had a name like wikipedia, and the function within it was something like wikifinder. The full implementation process is shown in this video (in Portuguese): YouTube – start at 17:30.

In another video, I demonstrate a test of the same model using three identical queries. In the first two attempts, the response includes <REDACTED>, while in the third attempt, the response is correct and unredacted. This segment can be viewed at minute 9:10.

Although both videos are in Portuguese, non-Portuguese speakers can follow the issue by observing the visual flow and output behavior.

After extensive testing, I isolated the root cause: when the names of Action Groups or their functions contain terms that are likely to appear in the model’s final output, such as “Wikipedia,” Bedrock may treat these as sensitive or system-reserved, leading to unintentional redaction.

Test Matrix: Naming Combinations and Their Results (Updated)

To verify the behavior, we tested the query:

“Give me the URL of the Wikipedia page about Pelé”

All test cases were executed using a with updated naming conventions. The previous agent had already been , ensuring that results reflect the current AWS infrastructure and configuration.

All tests were conducted using a newly configured Bedrock agent. The previous agent was deleted prior to testing, which isolates the outcome to current platform behavior, unaffected by legacy configurations or naming history.

To eliminate the redaction issue, avoid using names in Action Groups or functions that contain terms likely to appear in the model’s final response — such as “wikipedia” in this case. Doing so prevents unintended redactions and ensures consistent response accuracy.

This type of issue is a classic example of an intermittent, undocumented behavior that can lead to hours of debugging. If you’re encountering unexpected <REDACTED> tokens in your Bedrock agent responses, I strongly encourage you to examine your Action Group and function names.

My goal in sharing this is to help others avoid the same pitfall and to contribute to the growing community building solutions on Amazon Bedrock. If you’ve encountered similar quirks, please share them — collective knowledge is our best tool for navigating these edge cases.

If you found this guide helpful, stay connected for more insights on AI, cloud security, and AWS automation:

items.title: Amazon Aurora DSQL, the fastest serverless distributed SQL database is now generally available
items.author: Channy Yun (윤석찬)
items.content:
Today, we’re announcing the general availability of Amazon Aurora DSQL, the fastest serverless distributed SQL database with virtually unlimited scale, the highest availability, and zero infrastructure management for always available applications. You can remove the operational burden of patching, upgrades, and maintenance downtime and count on an easy-to-use developer experience to create a new database in a few quick steps.

When we introduced the preview of Aurora DSQL at AWS re:Invent 2024, our customers were excited by this innovative solution to simplify complex relational database challenges. In his keynote, Dr. Werner Vogels, CTO of Amazon.com, talked about managing complexity upfront in the design of Aurora DSQL. Unlike most traditional databases, Aurora DSQL is disaggregated into multiple independent components such as a query processor, adjudicator, journal, and crossbar.

These components have high cohesion, communicate through well-specified APIs, and scale independently based on your workloads. This architecture enables multi-Region strong consistency with low latency and globally synchronized time. To learn more about how Aurora DSQL works behind the scenes, watch Dr. Werner Vogels’ keynote and read about an Aurora DSQL story.

The architecture of Amazon Aurora DSQL Your application can use the fastest distributed SQL reads and writes and scale to meet any workload demand without any database sharding or instance upgrades. With Aurora DSQL, its active-active distributed architecture is designed for 99.99 percent availability in a single Region and 99.999 percent availability across multiple Regions. This means your applications can continue to read and write with strong consistency, even in the rare case an application is unable to connect to a Region cluster endpoint.

In a single-Region configuration, Aurora DSQL commits all write transactions to a distributed transaction log and synchronously replicates all committed log data to user storage replicas in three Availability Zones. Cluster storage replicas are distributed across a storage fleet and automatically scale to ensure optimal read performance.

Multi-Region clusters provide the same resilience and connectivity as single-Region clusters while improving availability through two Regional endpoints, one for each peered cluster Region. Both endpoints of a peered cluster present a single logical database and support concurrent read and write operations with strong data consistency. A third Region acts as a log-only witness which means there is is no cluster resource or endpoint. This means you can balance applications and connections for geographic locations, performance, or resiliency purposes, making sure readers consistently see the same data.

Aurora DSQL is an ideal choice to support applications using microservices and event-driven architectures, and you can design highly scalable solutions for industries such as banking, ecommerce, travel, and retail. It’s also ideal for multi-tenant software as a service (SaaS) applications and data-driven services like payment processing, gaming platforms, and social media applications that require multi-Region scalability and resilience.

Getting started with Amazon Aurora DSQL Aurora DSQL provides a easy-to-use experience, starting with a simple console experience. You can use familiar SQL clients to leverage existing skillsets, and integration with other AWS services to improve managing databases.

To create an Aurora DSQL cluster, go to the Aurora DSQL console and choose . You can choose either or configuration options to help you establish the right database infrastructure for your needs.

1. Create a single-Region cluster

To create a single-Region cluster, you only choose . That’s all.

To get the authentication token, choose and in your cluster detail page. Copy the endpoint from and the generated authentication token after is chosen in the Authentication token (Password) section.

Then, choose , and with a few clicks, you can seamlessly connect to your cluster.

After you connect the Aurora DSQL cluster, test your cluster by running sample SQL statements. You can also query SQL statements for your applications using your favorite programming languages: Python, Java, JavaScript, C++, Ruby, .NET, Rust, and Golang. You can build sample applications using a Django, Ruby on Rails, and AWS Lambda application to interact with Amazon Aurora DSQL.

2. Create a multi-Region cluster

To create the first cluster, choose in the console. You will also be required to choose the , which receives data written to any peered Region but doesn’t have an endpoint. Choose . If you already have a remote Region cluster, you can optionally enter its ARN.

Next, add an existing remote cluster or create your second cluster in another Region by choosing .

Now, you can create the second cluster with your peer cluster ARN as the first cluster.

When the second cluster is created, you must peer the cluster in in order to complete the multi-Region creation.

Go to the first cluster page and choose to confirm cluster peering for both clusters.

Now, your multi-Region cluster is created successfully. You can see details about the peers that are in other Regions in the tab.

To get hands-on experience with Aurora DSQL, you can use this step-by-step workshop. It walks through the architecture, key considerations, and best practices as you build a sample retail rewards point application with active-active resiliency.

What did we add after the preview?We used your feedback and suggestions during the preview period to add new capabilities. We’ve highlighted a few of the new features and capabilities:

  • –We improved your cluster management experience to create and peer multi-Region clusters as well as easily connect using AWS CloudShell.
  • – We added support for views, unique secondary indexes for tables with existing data and launched Auto-Analyze which removes the need to manually maintain accurate table statistics. Learn about Aurora DSQL PostgreSQL-compatible features.
  • Integration with AWS services –We integrated various AWS services such as AWS Backup for a full snapshot backup and Aurora DSQL cluster restore, AWS PrivateLink for private network connectivity, AWS CloudFormation for managing Aurora DSQL resources, and AWS CloudTrail for logging Aurora DSQL operations.

Aurora DSQL now provides a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server to improve developer productivity by making it easy for your generative AI models and database to interact through natural language. For example, install Amazon Q Developer CLI and configure Aurora DSQL MCP server. Amazon Q Developer CLI now has access to an Aurora DSQL cluster. You can easily explore the schema of your database, understand the structure of the tables, and even execute complex SQL queries, all without having to write any additional integration code.

Amazon Aurora DSQL is available today in the AWS US East (N. Virginia), US East (Ohio), US West (Oregon) Regions for single- and multi-Region clusters (two peers and one witness Region), Asia Pacific (Osaka) and Asia Pacific (Tokyo) for single-Region clusters, and Europe (Ireland), Europe (London), and Europe (Paris) for single-Region clusters.

You’re billed on a monthly basis using a single normalized billing unit called Distributed Processing Unit (DPU) for all request-based activity such as read/write. Storage is based on the total size of your database and measured in GB-months. You are only charged for one logical copy of your data per single-Region cluster or multi-Region peered cluster. As a part of the AWS Free Tier, your first 100,000 DPUs and 1 GB-month of storage each month is free. To learn more, visit Amazon Aurora DSQL Pricing.
